agent1_raw: The staffing reduction decision is the wrong question for this model. The retrospective study measures whether the model detects confirmed incidents faster than manual monitoring. The staffing question is whether model silence is safe to rely on when reduced overnight staffing means no one is watching cameras continuously. These are fundamentally different questions. A model that detects 90% of archived incidents still creates a 10% miss rate under reduced-staffing conditions, and for overnight wildfire ignitions, even a single undetected small fire can cause catastrophic losses before dawn. The logged incident recall figure is not the relevant safety metric for the staffing decision.
agent2_raw: Four issues make the staffing reduction proposal unjustifiable on current evidence. First, confirmed incident logs exclude small, self-extinguishing, or obscured ignitions — the model's recall is measured on incidents that human monitoring would have detected, not on the full set of operationally relevant overnight starts. Second, model silence + reduced staffing creates periods of no monitoring, which is not the same as model-aided monitoring — the prospective question is about detection gaps during silence, which the retrospective study doesn't measure. Third, camera upgrades and environmental changes create distribution shift. Fourth, average detection metrics don't address the tail risk of a missed overnight ignition. The cost asymmetry (catastrophic miss vs. nuisance false positive) requires conservatism, not average-case reasoning.
agent3_raw: The agency wants to reduce overnight staffing because the model flags incidents faster than manual monitoring on archived data. But the proposal's safety depends on what happens when the model is silent — and silent periods with reduced staffing mean no continuous human oversight. The archived incident study cannot answer this question because those incidents were all eventually detected under full staffing. The unlogged-start problem is equally critical: small fires that self-extinguished under full monitoring may represent exactly the overnight ignitions that reduced staffing with model reliance would miss. The asymmetric cost of a missed overnight fire makes conservative deployment (augmentation, not replacement) the only defensible near-term choice.
synthesis_raw: All three assessors reach critique_wins. The core issue — identified by all three — is that the staffing counterfactual is wrong: retrospective detection speed on archived incidents does not measure the miss risk when model silence replaces continuous human attention. The unlogged-start problem is identified by all three as the most important gap. Asymmetric error cost requiring conservative deployment is noted by all three. Camera and environment shift is noted by two of three. ETD: condition = parallel season with maintained staffing, simulating reduced-watch operations from model silence/alert patterns; supports_critique_if = simulated reduced-watch would have delayed first awareness beyond preset tolerance for any meaningful number of overnight ignitions or near-miss starts; supports_defense_if = simulated reduced-watch shows no delays beyond tolerance on logged and adjudicated near-miss starts; ambiguous_if = model performs well on logged fires but agency cannot credibly estimate miss rate for small/transient overnight ignitions.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Logged incidents miss unobserved small starts — recall on confirmed logs does not capture true miss risk for unlogged overnight ignitions
Issue 2: Model silence changes staffing counterfactual — retrospective detection speed does not measure delay risk when reduced staffing relies on model silence
Issue 3: Camera upgrades and environmental changes (vegetation, tourism, construction haze) create prospective distribution shift
Issue 4: Average detection metrics do not capture asymmetric cost of missed overnight ignitions — tail risk requires worst-case analysis, not average performance

empirical_test.condition: Parallel season maintaining current staffing while logging model alerts and silences, then simulating what reduced-watch operations would have meant for detection timing on all ignitions including near-miss overnight starts
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: In a parallel season where current staffing is maintained, simulated reduced-watch operations based on model silence would have delayed first awareness beyond the agency's preset tolerance for any meaningful number of overnight ignitions or near-miss starts
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: In a parallel season with maintained staffing, simulated reduced-watch operations show no delays beyond the preset tolerance on logged and independently adjudicated near-miss overnight starts while materially lowering nuisance-monitoring burden
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The model performs well on logged fires, but the agency cannot credibly estimate how often silent periods would miss small or transient overnight ignitions that humans might have noticed
